mnemonics: BALLS Contain Bounds: A Bounded set is one that can be 'Ball-ed' (completely contained inside some open ball of finite radius).
keyPoints: A set S in a metric space (X, d) is bounded if there exists a real number M > 0 and a point a in X such that d(a, x) ≤ M for all x in S.
Equivalently, a set is bounded if its diameter (the supremum of the distances between any two points in the set) is finite.
A subset of a bounded set is also bounded. The union of finitely many bounded sets is bounded.
commonMistakes: Confusing a 'bounded' set with a 'totally bounded' set. Total boundedness is a stronger condition that requires the set to be coverable by finitely many epsilon-balls for any epsilon > 0.

Assuming that if a set is closed and bounded, then it is compact. This is only true in specific metric spaces, like R^n with the Euclidean metric (Heine-Borel Theorem), but not in general metric spaces.
